ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for pesto pasta with Parma ham and ricotta specifically; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for pesto pasta with Parma ham and ricotta.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Pasta dish' is the closest parent; no specific concept for pesto pasta with Parma ham and ricotta.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of pesto pasta with Parma ham and ricotta, approximately 350 g, as typically served at an Italian trattoria.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with pesto sauce (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA entries for prosciutto (cured ham) and ricotta cheese
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for this exact composite dish. Values estimated by weighted-ingredient modelling: approximately 55% cooked pasta, 15% basil pesto, 15% Parma ham, 15% ricotta cheese, using USDA SR Legacy entries for each component. USDA FDC IDs used: cooked pasta (NDB 20121), basil pesto (FDC 172237), prosciutto/cured ham (FDC 174608), ricotta whole-milk (FDC 170851). Energy cross-checked with Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked al dente, wheat-based) GI ~45; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 applied from peer-reviewed GI tables for cooked wheat pasta (al dente). Pesto, Parma ham, and ricotta contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates and are expected to lower the overall GI slightly via fat and protein co-ingestion; GI 45 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate for this dish. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 32.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Parma ham and pesto (approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from cured Parma ham and salted pesto; if consumed, portion should be restricted to half a standard serving and paired with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat pasta carbohydrates, partially mitigated by fat and protein from pesto, Parma ham, and ricotta
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions (approximately 150–200 g) and choose al dente cooking to preserve the lower GI of pasta; the fat and protein content from ricotta and Parma ham help blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ricotta and Parma ham combined with high sodium from cured meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its saturated fat content (~3.1 g/100 g) and high sodium from Parma ham; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable, and the olive oil and pine nuts in pesto provide beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pesto Paradiso is an Italian pasta dish served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore, composed of rotondi (short round pasta) tossed in Genovese basil pesto, topped with thinly sliced Rotondi Parma ham (prosciutto di Parma) and a generous addition of fresh ricotta cheese. The dish originates from the culinary traditions of Liguria (pesto Genovese) and Emilia-Romagna (Parma ham), combined in a restaurant format popular in Singapore's Italian dining scene. The pasta is boiled al dente and coated in a vibrant green pesto made from fresh basil, pine nuts, Parmesan, garlic, and extra-virgin olive oil. The Parma ham contributes a delicate, salty, and slightly sweet cured-meat flavour, while the ricotta adds a creamy, mild, and slightly grainy texture that balances the richness of the pesto. The overall flavour profile is herbaceous, savoury, and creamy with a pleasant saltiness from the cured ham. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~198 kcal/100 g), meaningful protein from the ham and ricotta (~9.2 g/100 g), and moderate fat (~9.8 g/100 g) with a favourable proportion of monounsaturated fats from olive oil and pine nuts. The glycemic index is low (~45) due to the al dente pasta preparation and the fat-protein buffering from the other components, making it a moderate glycemic load meal. Sodium content is elevated due to the Parma ham and pesto, which is a clinical consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets. It contains gluten, dairy, and tree nuts (pine nuts) as major allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, USDA, ICMR-NIN) contains an exact entry for this composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-ingredient modelling using USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for each component (cooked pasta, basil pesto, prosciutto, ricotta). GI is estimated from peer-reviewed pasta GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-ingredient model (55% cooked pasta, 15% basil pesto, 15% Parma ham, 15% ricotta) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. Actual restaurant values may differ based on portion ratios and recipe specifics. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component entries used for this estimation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats from dairy (ricotta) and cured meat; this is a trace level. 4. Glycemic load per serving is 32.3 g. 5. This dish is not suitable for halal diets due to the presence of Parma ham (pork product). 6. The dish is sold at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, Boat Quay, Singapore, at SGD 40.11 per serving. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used.

Built weighted-ingredient nutrient model from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries.
Applied GI of 45 from peer-reviewed pasta GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021) with analogous estimate documentation.
